Fix REAL defects in a generated HLD session. Workspace: /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/workspace/657589a4-60b0-4d99-b932-09b3f6da738f_hld/_response.md (+ maybe capacity.py). Fix EACH directly in _response.md (and capacity.py if math is wrong, re-running with /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/.venv/bin/python): 1. [MAJOR] §5.2 (line 174) vs §6.2 component table (line 262), §8 Great-tier (line 559), §11 (line 720): Internal contradiction on the most-emphasized path (read hot-path + CDN offload). The redirect API contract emits `Cache-Control: private, max-age=0` on the 302, which forbids ALL caching by shared caches (CDN) and even the browser. Yet the design repeatedly claims the CDN edge-caches the 302 to offload origin traffic: 'Edge caching a 302 means short max-age, so we still see most clicks' (line 262), 'A CDN edge-caches the 302 briefly to shave origin load' (line 559), 'the CDN absorbs edge-cacheable redirects' (line 720). With `private, max-age=0` the CDN delivers ZERO redirect offload, so the entire CDN tier's stated purpose (absorbing a chunk of the 580K/s) is contradicted by the chosen header. An interviewer on this exact topic would catch it immediately. FIX: Pick one coherent story. If CDN offload is wanted: set `Cache-Control: public, max-age=` and explicitly acknowledge you go blind to clicks within that window (the analytics cost of edge-caching a 302). If full per-click analytics is wanted: keep `private, max-age=0` and stop claiming the CDN offloads redirects — its value then is only TLS termination / DDoS / proxy-hop latency, not cache-hit offload. Make the header and the prose agree. CRITICAL for any mermaid finding: rewrite offending node/edge labels to remove ALL breaking characters (parens, colons, semicolons, slashes, quotes, angle/curly braces) — replace ":" and ";" with words, drop parens, use
for line breaks; keep flowchart TD. Keep all 11 sections, md > 20,000 chars, no LaTeX, scope-lock-first §1. Re-scan mermaid after fixing. Return what changed, final mdChars, final mermaidCount.

1	# Design a URL Shortener (TinyURL / Bitly)
2	
3	> One line frame: we take a long URL, hand back a short code, and on every click we redirect that code back to the original — at a roughly 100:1 read-to-write ratio, so this is overwhelmingly a **read hot-path** problem. The whole game is making the redirect fast, cheap, and never-wrong, while generating short codes that *never collide*.
4	
5	---
6	
7	## 1. Scope, Requirements & Assumptions
8	
9	> 🎙️ "Here's how I'll structure this: I'll lock scope with a couple of quick assumptions, state the requirements and rough scale, sketch the API and data model, draw the high-level design one requirement at a time, then go deep on the two or three hardest parts — short-code generation, the read hot-path, and the analytics pipeline — checking in with you as I go. Since this is an Amazon-style bar, I'll keep cost and operations first-class and work backwards from the customer. Sound good?"
10	
11	> 💬 "The core tension is simple to state and hard to nail: this thing is read-dominated by ~100:1, so I'll spend almost my entire budget making the redirect cheap, and I'll obsess over one correctness invariant — short codes must be unique *by construction*, never by luck."
12	
13	### 🔒 Scope lock (the architecture-forking assumptions — veto any of these)
14	
15	I'll state the few questions whose answers *fork the whole design* as assumptions you can stop me on:
16	
17	1. **Reads dominate ~100:1 and we're a single-region write tier to start.** Almost all traffic is redirects; creates are rare. *— stop me if writes are actually heavy or you want active-active multi-region writes from day one.*
18	2. **Short-code generation must be collision-free by construction, not by retry.** I'll assume we cannot tolerate "generate-then-check-then-maybe-retry" loops at write scale. *— stop me if a tiny collision-retry rate is acceptable.*
19	3. **Custom aliases are in scope** (e.g. `/my-brand`), so the code namespace is shared between auto-generated and user-chosen codes. *— stop me if custom aliases are out; that removes a whole collision class.*
20	4. **Analytics is "good enough" eventual, not exact.** Click counts can lag a few seconds and we tolerate at-least-once counting with dedup. *— stop me if you need exact, real-time, billable click counts.*
21	
22	> 🤝 **In/out-of-scope checkpoint:** "In scope: shorten, redirect, custom alias, async click analytics. Out: user accounts/billing UI, link editing/preview pages, malware/phishing scanning, A-B link rotation. Add or cut before I commit?"
23	
24	### Functional requirements (derived from the locked assumptions — "above the line")
25	
26	Since we assumed a shared, collision-free namespace with custom aliases and heavy reads, the core flows are:
27	
28	1. **Shorten a long URL → short code.** `POST` a long URL, get back `https://sho.rt/aZ3kP9`.
29	   - 🗣️ **Plain words:** someone pastes a giant URL and we hand them a tiny one that points to the same place.
30	   - ⚡ **Why it's hard:** the short code must be globally unique *without* a slow check-then-retry on every write, and it must coexist with user-chosen custom aliases in the same namespace.
31	2. **Redirect short code → long URL.** `GET /aZ3kP9` returns an HTTP redirect to the original.
32	   - 🗣️ **Plain words:** when anyone clicks the short link, their browser instantly lands on the real page.
33	   - ⚡ **Why it's hard:** this is ~580K requests/s at peak; it must be single-digit-millisecond and survive a cache flush — and the 301-vs-302 choice silently decides whether analytics even works.
34	3. **Custom alias.** Let a user request a specific code (`/spring-sale`) if it's free.
35	   - 🗣️ **Plain words:** a brand can pick its own memorable short link instead of random characters.
36	   - ⚡ **Why it's hard:** custom aliases and auto-generated codes share one namespace, so we need an atomic "claim if free" that can't let two creators win the same alias.
37	4. **Async click analytics.** Count clicks per code (and rough geo/referrer) without slowing the redirect.
38	   - 🗣️ **Plain words:** the link owner can see how many people clicked and roughly from where.
39	   - ⚡ **Why it's hard:** we cannot do a synchronous DB write on the 580K/s hot path — counting has to ride a fire-and-forget pipeline that's lossy-tolerant but de-duplicated.
40	
41	**Below the line (out of scope):** user auth/billing, link editing & expiry-management UI, branded-domain onboarding, phishing/malware scanning, QR codes, link preview/unfurl, A-B rotation.
42	
43	### Non-Functional requirements (PROVISIONAL targets, tagged to the assumption they depend on)
44	
45	1. **Redirect availability ≥ 99.99% ("four nines").** *(depends on assumption 1: read-dominated)*
46	   - 🗣️ **Plain words:** the redirect path is down less than ~52 minutes per *year*, total.
47	   - 💥 **What breaks without it:** every existing short link everywhere — in emails, tweets, printed QR codes — silently 404s; the redirect is the product, so an outage is total.
48	2. **Redirect p99 ≤ ~50ms server-side on a cache hit (sub-10ms typical).** *(depends on assumption 1)*
49	   - 🗣️ **Plain words:** 99 out of 100 clicks resolve in well under a tenth of a second.
50	   - 💥 **What breaks without it:** slow redirects feel like broken links; users abandon and link owners stop using us.
51	3. **Code uniqueness = strong/exact (zero collisions).** *(depends on assumption 2 + 3)*
52	   - 🗣️ **Plain words:** no two long URLs ever get the same short code, and no custom alias ever overwrites someone else's.
53	   - 💥 **What breaks without it:** a collision sends a user to the *wrong* site — a correctness and trust catastrophe (think: a banking link resolving to a phishing page).
54	4. **Analytics: eventual, at-least-once + dedup, lag ≤ a few seconds.** *(depends on assumption 4)*
55	   - 🗣️ **Plain words:** click counts are allowed to be a few seconds stale and we dedup retries so we don't over-count.
56	   - 💥 **What breaks without it:** either we slow the hot path (synchronous counting) or we lose/double-count clicks and owners stop trusting the dashboard.
57	
58	> ⚠️ **Gaps I'd flag out loud:** if click analytics ever becomes *billable* (pay-per-click), eventual at-least-once isn't good enough and I'd need an exactly-once, reconciled counting tier — I'll revisit this in the ledger.
59	
60	> 🎙️ **Script:** "So the product is four flows: shorten, redirect, custom alias, and async analytics. The redirect is the whole ballgame — it's ~100x the write traffic, so I want it on a cache at single-digit milliseconds and four-nines available. The one correctness rule I will not bend is that short codes are unique by construction — a collision means sending someone to the wrong site, which is unacceptable. Analytics I'll treat as eventual and lossy-tolerant so it never touches the hot path. Everything else — accounts, billing, malware scanning — I'm putting below the line unless you want it."
61	
62	---
63	
64	## 2. The Forks Behind My Assumptions (what changes if I'm wrong)
65	
66	These are the residual forks from the scope lock — the three places my design is most sensitive. I'm not re-asking scale (I've committed to ~100:1 reads and single-region writes); I'm exposing where the architecture would *bend*.
67	
68	**Fork A — Code generation strategy (I locked "collision-free by construction").**
69	- ↔️ **What changes if I'm wrong:**
70	  - If a small collision-retry rate is fine → I can use a **random base62 / hash-prefix** generator with a DB uniqueness check and retry-on-collision, no global coordinator at all.
71	  - If it truly must be zero-collision and zero-retry → I need a **counter + base62** scheme behind a global ID allocator (a key-generation service handing out pre-minted ranges), which I'll defend as the primary.
72	- 🗣️ **How I'd say it:** "I'm assuming we want codes that are unique without a check-and-retry loop, so I'll use a counter. If you're OK with a one-in-a-billion retry on write, random codes are simpler — tell me and I'll switch."
73	
74	**Fork B — 301 vs 302 redirect (I locked "analytics must work, so we lean 302").**
75	- ↔️ **What changes if I'm wrong:**
76	  - If SEO/permanence and offloading traffic from our servers matter more than per-click analytics → use **301 (permanent)**: browsers and intermediaries cache it, so we serve fewer redirects, but we *stop seeing most clicks*.
77	  - If analytics and the ability to change the destination matter more → use **302 (temporary / found)**: every click comes back to us, so we count every click and can repoint a link, at the cost of more redirect traffic.
78	- 🗣️ **How I'd say it:** "I'll default to 302 so every click hits us and analytics is real — if you'd rather offload traffic and don't care about click data, we flip to 301 and accept that browsers stop telling us about clicks."
79	
80	**Fork C — Analytics accuracy (I locked "eventual, at-least-once + dedup").**
81	- ↔️ **What changes if I'm wrong:**
82	  - If counts are *display-only* → an async fire-and-forget pipeline with approximate aggregation is plenty (cheapest).
83	  - If counts become *billable/exact* → I need durable ingest (a log we can replay), idempotent consumers keyed on an event id, and periodic reconciliation — a meaningfully heavier pipeline.
84	- 🗣️ **How I'd say it:** "If clicks are just a vanity dashboard, my async pipeline is fine; if you're charging customers per click, I'll harden it to exactly-once with reconciliation."
85	
86	> 🤝 **Checkpoint:** "Those are the three forks I care about — generation strategy, 301-vs-302, and analytics accuracy. Want to lock any of them differently before I size capacity?"
87	
88	---
89	
90	## 3. Scale & Capacity (talkable numbers)
91	
92	I computed these with a small `capacity.py` rather than hand-waving. **QPS** = queries per second (requests hitting us each second). **TTL** = time-to-live, how long a cached value lives before it expires. I derive a **peak** from the daily average with a stated **burst factor** of 5x (diurnal swing + viral link spikes), and I size the read tier from **miss QPS** (the fraction of reads that escape the cache and hit the DB), not from cache size.
93	
94	| Metric | Derivation (step-by-step) | Rounded | Talkable phrase | Decision it drives |
95	|---|---|---|---|---|
96	| New links/day | assume 100M creates/day (Bitly-class) | 100M/day | "a hundred million new links a day" | sets write tier + keyspace |
97	| Write QPS (peak) | 100M ÷ 86,400 ≈ 1.2K/s avg; ×5 burst | ~6K/s | "about six thousand writes a second at peak" | a single Postgres primary handles this — writes are *not* the problem |
98	| Read QPS (peak) | 100M × 100 = 10B reads/day; ÷86,400 ≈ 116K/s avg; ×5 burst | **~580K/s** | "about half a million redirects a second at peak" | **THE decision-forcing number** |
99	| Cache miss QPS (peak) | 580K/s × (1 − 0.95 hit) | ~29K/s | "about thirty thousand misses a second" | sizes DB read replicas, not cache |
100	| Cold-cache QPS (0% hit) | full 580K/s hits DB | ~580K/s | "if the cache is cold, half a million land on the DB" | forces gradual cache warm-up + request coalescing |
101	| Redis nodes | 580K/s ÷ ~150K ops/s per node | ~4 (round up + headroom) | "four-ish cache nodes" | shard/replicate the cache |
102	| DB read replicas | 29K miss/s ÷ ~10K reads/s per replica | ~3 | "about three read replicas" | replica fan-out for misses |
103	| Keyspace (base62) | 62^7 ≈ 3.5T; 62^6 ≈ 57B | **7 chars** | "seven characters gives three-and-a-half trillion codes" | code length = 7 |
104	| Mapping table (5yr) | 182B rows × ~285B × idx 1.6 × repl 3 × headroom 1.5 | ~375TB | "a few hundred terabytes" | shard the mapping store |
105	| Click-events table (90d raw) | 10B/day × 90d × 100B × repl 3 | **~270TB** | "the biggest table by far" | drives the data-lifecycle / partition-drop story |
106	| Cost (rough) | redis ~$8K + db ~$10K + analytics ~$25K + egress/LB ~$150K | **~$190K/mo** | "around two hundred K a month" | egress/LB dominates, not the DB |
107	| Unit economics | $190K ÷ 300B reqs/mo | **~$0.64 / 1M redirects** | "under a dollar per million clicks" | the number I'd put on a one-pager |
108	
109	**Keyspace math (plain text):** base62 = digits + lower + upper = 62 symbols. 62^6 ≈ 57 billion (too tight against 182B links over 5 years), 62^7 ≈ 3.5 trillion (comfortable headroom even at our 182B). **So 7 characters.**
110	
111	**The ONE decision-forcing number + flip threshold:** ~**580K reads/s at peak**. A single Redis node tops out around **150K ops/s** (it's effectively single-threaded). 580K ÷ 150K ≈ 4, so the cache *must* be sharded/replicated — a single cache node is impossible. **Flip threshold:** if peak reads ever fell below ~150K/s (e.g. a private/internal shortener), I'd collapse to one cache node and skip sharding entirely.
112	
113	> 🎙️ **Script:** "The number that drives everything is about half a million redirects a second at peak — that's a hundred-to-one over writes, which are only about six thousand a second. A single cache node maxes out near a hundred-fifty thousand ops a second, so I have to spread reads across a handful of cache nodes; writes I'll barely think about. Storage is a few hundred terabytes for the mapping and the bigger worry is the click-events table at a couple hundred terabytes — I'll partition-drop that. And interestingly the biggest *cost* line isn't the database, it's the load-balancer and bandwidth for serving billions of tiny redirects — about two hundred K a month total, under a dollar per million clicks."
114	
115	---
116	
117	## 4. Core Entities
118	
119	This is a first-draft scaffold; full fields land in §7 (Data Model).
120	
121	- **ShortLink** — the mapping. Owns: `short_code → long_url`, owner, created/expires timestamps, and whether it's a custom alias. It's the heart of the system.
122	  - 🗣️ **Analogy:** like a single line in a phone book — a short name on the left, the real number on the right.
123	  - ⚠️ **Probe:** "How do you guarantee the short_code is unique?" → it's the primary key; auto-generated codes come from a counter (unique by construction), custom aliases use an atomic insert-if-absent so a duplicate just fails.
124	- **Counter / ID range** — the global allocator state behind auto-generated codes (a monotonically increasing number we base62-encode).
125	  - 🗣️ **Analogy:** like a deli ticket dispenser — every customer gets the next number, nobody shares one.
126	  - ⚠️ **Probe:** "Isn't that a single bottleneck?" → no, the allocator hands out *ranges* (e.g. 1,000 IDs) to each app server, so servers mint codes locally and only touch the allocator once per range.
127	- **ClickEvent** — one record per redirect for analytics: code, timestamp, coarse geo/referrer/user-agent.
128	  - 🗣️ **Analogy:** like a turnstile counter — every time someone walks through (clicks), it ticks.
129	  - ⚠️ **Probe:** "Won't writing this slow every redirect?" → it never blocks the redirect; we fire the event onto a queue and a separate worker aggregates it.
130	- **Owner / Account** *(thin, mostly out of scope)* — who created the link, for authz and per-owner listing.
131	  - 🗣️ **Analogy:** like the name on a library card — it just says whose link this is.
132	  - ⚠️ **Probe:** "Can someone read another user's link analytics?" → object-level authz: we check the caller's id from their token matches the link's `owner_id`.
133	
134	> 🎙️ **Script:** "There are really four entities. The star is the ShortLink — code to long URL, that's the whole product. Behind it is a Counter that mints unique numbers we base62-encode into codes. ClickEvents are the analytics records, and they ride a queue so they never touch the redirect. There's a thin Owner for authz. I'll flesh out exact fields and keys in the data model, but the surprising one is the Counter — it sounds like a single bottleneck, and a big part of the design is making sure it isn't."
135	
136	---
137	
138	## 5. API / Interface
139	
140	Everything is REST under `/v1`. **VERSIONING:** prefix all paths with `/v1`; additive changes (a new optional field) don't bump the version, breaking changes get `/v2`. **PAGINATION:** any list endpoint (e.g. a user's links, or analytics rows) uses a **cursor/keyset token**, not offset/limit — offset gets slower as it skips more rows and can skip or duplicate rows when data shifts under it. **SECURITY:** the caller is identified from a **JWT** *(a signed token in the request header that proves who the user is — we never trust a client-supplied user id)*; every mutating or owner-scoped endpoint does **object-level authz** ("does this caller own *this* link?"), not just "are they logged in?".
141	
142	### 5.1 Shorten a URL — `POST /v1/links`
143	- **Why POST:** it creates new server state (a new mapping), so POST, not GET.
144	- **Key fields:**
145	  - `longUrl: string` — the destination; server validates it's a well-formed http(s) URL.
146	  - `customAlias: string?` — optional; if present we attempt to claim exactly that code.
147	  - `expiresAt: string?` — optional TTL for the link.
148	  - `Idempotency-Key: <uuid>` (header) — so a client retry after a network blip doesn't mint two different codes for the same intent.
149	  - *Absent on purpose:* `shortCode` for the auto case (the **server** mints it — clients can't pick random codes); `ownerId` (taken from the JWT, never the body, so no one can create links as someone else).
150	```
151	// Request
152	POST /v1/links
153	Idempotency-Key: 7c1f...e9
154	{ "longUrl": "https://example.com/very/long/path?utm=spring", "customAlias": "spring-sale", "expiresAt": "2026-12-31T00:00:00Z" }
155	
156	// Response 201
157	{ "shortCode": "spring-sale", "shortUrl": "https://sho.rt/spring-sale", "longUrl": "https://example.com/very/long/path?utm=spring", "createdAt": "2026-06-21T10:00:00Z" }
158	
159	// Response 409 (alias taken)
160	{ "error": "ALIAS_TAKEN", "message": "spring-sale is already in use" }
161	```
162	- **Key decision — idempotency + alias claim:** the `Idempotency-Key` lets a retried create return the *same* code instead of a second one; the custom-alias path is an **atomic insert-if-absent** that returns 409 on conflict rather than overwriting.
163	
164	### 5.2 Redirect — `GET /{shortCode}`
165	- **Why GET + a redirect (not JSON):** browsers follow HTTP redirects natively; returning JSON would require client code, which defeats "paste a link anywhere."
166	- **Key fields:** just the path code. No body.
167	```
168	// Request
169	GET /aZ3kP9   Host: sho.rt
170	
171	// Response 302 (default — keeps analytics)
172	HTTP/1.1 302 Found
173	Location: https://example.com/very/long/path?utm=spring
174	Cache-Control: private, max-age=0
175	```
176	- **Key decision — 302 vs 301:** I return **302 Found** *(temporary redirect — the browser comes back to us next time)* by default so every click is observable for analytics and so we can repoint the link later. **301 Moved Permanently** *(browser caches the mapping and skips us)* would cut our traffic and cost but blind us to clicks; it's a per-product flip (Fork B). For a 404 (unknown code) we return a small branded page, not a stack trace.
177	
178	### 5.3 Custom alias — same `POST /v1/links` with `customAlias`
179	- Covered by 5.1; the only addition is the **409 ALIAS_TAKEN** contract. Object-level note: a custom alias must also pass a denylist (no impersonating reserved paths like `api`, `admin`, `login`).
180	
181	### 5.4 Read analytics — `GET /v1/links/{shortCode}/stats`
182	- **Why GET:** read-only.
183	- **Key fields:** `from`, `to` (time window), `cursor` (keyset pagination for raw rows).
184	```
185	// Request
186	GET /v1/links/aZ3kP9/stats?from=2026-06-01&to=2026-06-21&cursor=eyJ0cyI6...
187	
188	// Response 200
189	{ "shortCode": "aZ3kP9", "totalClicks": 184213, "byCountry": { "US": 90112, "IN": 41200 }, "windowFrom": "2026-06-01", "windowTo": "2026-06-21", "nextCursor": "eyJ0cyI6Li4ufQ" }
190	```
191	- **Key decision — object-level authz:** we check `caller.sub == link.ownerId` before returning stats; counts are served from a pre-aggregated store and are *eventually* consistent (a few seconds stale is fine).
192	
193	> 💬 "Notice the client never sends `ownerId` or, for the auto case, the `shortCode` — the server owns both. That's how we stop someone forging links as another user or grabbing a code they shouldn't."
194	
195	> 🎙️ **Script:** "Four endpoints. POST to create a link — with an idempotency key so a retry doesn't mint two codes, and the owner comes from the token, never the body. GET on the bare code is the redirect; I return a 302 by default so analytics keeps working. Custom alias reuses the create endpoint but can 409 if the alias is taken. And a stats endpoint that's owner-scoped with object-level authz and cursor pagination. Everything's under slash-v-one so I can evolve it without breaking old links."
196	
197	---
198	
199	## 6. High-Level Design (built one functional requirement at a time)
200	
201	Color convention (reused in every diagram): **blue = clients**, **green = our services (hot path)**, **amber = async/queues**, **grey = datastores/caches (cylinders)**, **purple = external**.
202	
203	### 6.1 "Shorten a long URL → short code"
204	
205	We're turning a long URL into a 7-char code that's unique *by construction*. The interesting part is the code generator and where its state lives.
206	
207	**Architecture decisions table**
208	
209	| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept |
210	|---|---|---|---|---|
211	| Write Service | Stateless app servers that handle `POST /v1/links` | Simple, horizontally scalable; writes are only ~6K/s so this is easy | Not a single big monolith node (no HA, can't scale a hot path independently) | More moving parts, need a load balancer in front |
212	| Key Generation Service (KGS) | A tiny service that hands each Write Service a *range* of pre-minted IDs (e.g. 1,000 numbers) from a durable counter | Makes codes unique by construction with almost no per-write coordination | Not random+check-and-retry (extra DB read per write, retries under load); not per-write counter calls (KGS becomes a 6K/s hot spot) | Burned/lost IDs when a server crashes mid-range — fine, the keyspace is 3.5T |
213	| Base62 encoder | Pure function: integer → 7-char `[0-9a-zA-Z]` string | Compact, URL-safe, deterministic; no DB round-trip | Not hashing the URL (hashes collide and need a check); not UUIDs (too long, ugly) | None meaningful |
214	| Mapping store (Postgres, sharded) | Relational DB holding `short_code → long_url`, sharded by `short_code` | Need an atomic insert-if-absent for custom aliases and a strong PK uniqueness guarantee | Not a plain KV without conditional writes (can't cleanly enforce alias uniqueness); not one unsharded DB (375TB won't fit) | Cross-shard queries are hard, so we denormalize and key everything by code |
215	
216	> 🎙️ **Narration:** "The Write Service is just stateless app servers — writes are light so this is the boring part. The clever bit is the KGS: instead of asking a central counter for a number on every write, each server grabs a block of a thousand IDs at once and mints codes locally, so the counter is touched once per thousand creates, not once per create. I base62-encode the number into a 7-character code — base62 just means I have 62 symbols, the digits plus both cases of letters, so seven characters gives me trillions of codes. I considered hashing the URL or using random strings, but both can collide and force a check-and-retry on every write; the counter is unique by construction. The store is Postgres sharded by code because custom aliases need an atomic insert-if-absent that a dumb key-value store can't cleanly give me."
217	
218	> **🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
219	> **KGS (Key Generation Service)** — a small service that pre-allocates blocks of unique IDs from a durable counter. Here it lets every app server mint codes without hitting a central counter on each write.
220	> **base62** — encoding a number using 62 symbols (0-9, a-z, A-Z). Here it turns a big integer into a short, URL-safe code.
221	> **Insert-if-absent** — a DB write that succeeds only if the key doesn't already exist. Here it makes claiming a custom alias atomic so two creators can't both win.
222	> **Shard** — splitting one logical table across many DB machines by a key. Here we shard by `short_code` so reads and writes for a code always land on one machine.
223	
224	**Diagram — create flow**
225	```mermaid
226	flowchart TD
227	    Client[Client app]:::client
228	    Client -->|"1 POST v1 links"| LB[Load balancer]:::svc
229	    LB -->|"2 route"| WS[Write Service]:::svc
230	    WS -->|"3 need a code"| KGS[Key Generation Service]:::svc
231	    KGS -->|"4 grant ID range"| CounterDB[(Counter store)]:::db
232	    WS -->|"5 base62 encode locally"| WS
233	    WS -->|"6 insert mapping"| Mapping[(Mapping store sharded)]:::db
234	    WS -->|"7 return shortUrl"| Client
235	    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
236	    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
237	    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
238	```
239	↳ reuses existing: none (first slice)
240	
241	**Numbered step narration**
242	1. **Client POSTs the long URL** to the load balancer. Why LB: it spreads ~6K writes/s and gives HA. State: none yet. What goes wrong: a network retry — handled by the `Idempotency-Key`.
243	2. **LB routes to a Write Service** instance. Why: stateless services are interchangeable. State: none.
244	3. **Write Service asks KGS for capacity** *only if its local range is exhausted*. Why: amortizes the counter. State: server about to receive a new range.
245	4. **KGS atomically bumps the durable counter** and grants a range (e.g. IDs 5,001,000–5,001,999). Why: one atomic increment per 1,000 creates. State: counter advanced; range is now "owned" by that server. What goes wrong: server crash burns the range — acceptable, 3.5T keyspace.
246	5. **Write Service base62-encodes the next ID locally.** Why: pure function, no I/O. State: a candidate code exists.
247	6. **Write Service inserts the mapping** (`code → long_url`) on the shard owned by that code. For a custom alias this is insert-if-absent → 409 on conflict. State: link is durable. What goes wrong: alias collision → 409 returned, no overwrite.
248	7. **Returns the short URL.** State: client has its link.
249	
250	> 💬 "The whole trick on writes is: don't call the counter per write — grab a block of a thousand and mint locally. The counter goes from a 6K/s hot spot to a 6-per-second nudge."
251	
252	> 🎙️ **Script:** "To create a link, a stateless write service grabs a code. It mints codes from a block of IDs the key-generation service handed it — base62-encoding a number — so the only correctness rule, uniqueness, is guaranteed by construction instead of by retrying. It writes the mapping into Postgres sharded by code, and for a custom alias it's an atomic insert-if-absent so two people can't claim the same alias. The trade-off is we sometimes waste a block of IDs when a server dies, but with trillions of codes that's free."
253	
254	### 6.2 "Redirect short code → long URL"
255	
256	This is the hot path: ~580K/s. The whole design is "answer from cache, almost never touch the DB." It builds directly on 6.1's mapping store.
257	
258	**Architecture decisions table**
259	
260	| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept |
261	|---|---|---|---|---|
262	| CDN / edge | Geographically distributed cache of redirect responses near users | Terminates most traffic near the user, cuts latency and origin load | Not origin-only (every click crosses the internet to us) | Edge caching a 302 means short max-age, so we still see most clicks |
263	| Redirect Service | Stateless app servers that resolve a code to a URL and emit a 302 | Tiny, fast, horizontally scalable | Not serving directly from the DB (DB can't do 580K/s point reads) | Another tier to operate |
264	| Redis cache (sharded) | In-memory store of `code → long_url`, sharded across nodes | 580K/s ÷ 150K/node ⇒ must shard; in-memory = sub-ms reads | Not a local in-process cache only (can't share across many servers, cold on deploy); not Memcached (Redis gives us richer ops + persistence options) | Cache and DB can briefly disagree; we use TTL + write-through |
265	| Mapping store (Postgres replicas) | Same store from 6.1, read replicas for cache misses | Misses are only ~29K/s ⇒ ~3 replicas absorb them | Not hitting the primary for reads (would compete with writes) | Replica lag (ms) — fine, mappings are immutable after create |
266	
267	> 🎙️ **Narration:** "On a click, I want to never touch the database. The request hits a CDN — a network of caches near the user — then a stateless redirect service, then Redis, an in-memory cache. Redis is in-memory so reads are sub-millisecond, and I shard it because one node tops out around a hundred-fifty thousand ops a second and I'm at half a million. Only the ~5% of reads that miss go to a Postgres read replica — that's about thirty thousand a second, which three replicas handle. I emit a 302 so the browser comes back next time and I keep seeing clicks. The trade-off is the cache and DB can disagree for a moment, but mappings are immutable once created, so that's a non-issue."
268	
269	> **🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
270	> **CDN** — a network of cache servers spread around the world that serve responses from near the user. Here it absorbs a chunk of redirect traffic before it ever reaches us.
271	> **Redis** — an in-memory key-value store with sub-millisecond reads. Here it holds the hot `code → URL` mappings so the DB is rarely touched.
272	> **Read replica** — a read-only copy of the database kept in sync with the primary. Here replicas serve the few cache misses so reads don't compete with writes.
273	> **302 Found** — an HTTP "temporary redirect" the browser doesn't permanently cache. Here it ensures every click comes back to us for analytics.
274	
275	**Diagram — redirect (hot) flow with miss fallback**
276	```mermaid
277	flowchart TD
278	    Client[Client app]:::client
279	    Client -->|"1 GET code"| CDN[CDN edge]:::ext
280	    CDN -->|"2 miss to origin"| RS[Redirect Service]:::svc
281	    RS -->|"3 read cache"| Redis[(Redis sharded)]:::db
282	    Redis -->|"4 hit return URL"| RS
283	    RS -->|"5 miss read replica"| Replica[(Postgres replica)]:::db
284	    Replica -->|"6 backfill cache"| Redis
285	    RS -->|"7 emit 302"| Client
286	    RS -.->|"8 fire click event"| Queue[[Click queue]]:::async
287	    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
288	    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
289	    classDef async fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
290	    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
291	    classDef ext fill:#3a2150,stroke:#9b59b6,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
292	```
293	↳ reuses existing: Mapping store (from §6.1)
294	
295	**Numbered step narration**
296	1. **Client GETs the code.** Why CDN first: closest cache wins. State: none. What goes wrong: CDN region down → falls back to origin.
297	2. **CDN miss goes to origin** (a stateless Redirect Service). Why: CDN can't hold every long-tail code. State: none.
298	3. **Redirect Service reads Redis** by code. Why: in-memory, sub-ms. State: none.
299	4. **Cache hit returns the URL** (~95% of the time). State: none. This is the common case.
300	5. **On miss, read a Postgres replica.** Why: replicas absorb the ~29K/s miss load. State: none. What goes wrong: a stampede on a cold popular key → request coalescing (single-flight), covered in §8.
301	6. **Backfill Redis** with the result (write-through on read) + TTL. State: code now hot in cache.
302	7. **Emit a 302** with the `Location`. State: browser redirects. What goes wrong: unknown code → 404 branded page.
303	8. **Fire a click event** onto the queue, *fire-and-forget* (dashed). Why: must not block the redirect. State: an event is enqueued for async counting.
304	
305	> 💬 "The redirect answers from memory and the database basically never sees a read — only the 5% of misses, about thirty thousand a second, which is nothing."
306	
307	> 🎙️ **Script:** "A click hits a CDN, then a stateless redirect service, then Redis — an in-memory cache that answers in under a millisecond. About 95% of reads are served from cache; only misses fall through to a Postgres read replica, which then backfills the cache. I emit a 302 so the browser keeps coming back and I keep seeing clicks, and I fire the click event onto a queue without waiting for it. The one decision that makes this slice work is sharding the cache, because one node can't do half a million reads a second."
308	
309	### 6.3 "Custom alias"
310	
311	Mostly reuses 6.1 — the new behavior is the **atomic claim** and the **shared namespace** with auto-generated codes. The risk is two creators racing for the same alias, or an alias colliding with a future auto code.
312	
313	**Architecture decisions table**
314	
315	| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept |
316	|---|---|---|---|---|
317	| Alias claim (unique PK insert) | The mapping insert uses the alias as the primary key; DB rejects a duplicate | DB-enforced uniqueness is the simplest correct serialization point | Not "read-then-write in app code" (classic race: both read free, both write) | A loser just retries with a different alias |
318	| Namespace partition | Auto codes are base62 of large counter IDs; reserved-word denylist for aliases | Keeps custom and auto codes from colliding by construction | Not a shared random pool (then auto and custom can clash) | Aliases can't look like a future auto code prefix — minor UX rule |
319	
320	> 🎙️ **Narration:** "Custom aliases share the same code namespace as auto codes, so I need the claim to be atomic. I just make the alias the primary key and let the database reject a duplicate — that's the cleanest serialization point, no application-side lock. The classic mistake is read-then-write: two users both check 'is it free?', both see yes, both write, one silently clobbers the other. To stop a custom alias ever colliding with a future auto code, auto codes always come from large counter integers and aliases live with a reserved denylist, so the two pools don't overlap."
321	
322	> **🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
323	> **Unique constraint** — a DB rule that no two rows share the same key. Here the alias is the PK, so a duplicate insert simply fails instead of overwriting.
324	> **Namespace collision** — when two different generation strategies produce the same code. Here we prevent it by keeping auto codes in the high-integer space and aliases gated by a denylist.
325	
326	↳ builds on §6.1 diagram — no new components; the new edge is the **409 on conflict** return from the Mapping store insert.
327	
328	**Numbered step narration (delta only)**
329	1. Same create path as 6.1 through step 6, but with `customAlias` as the code.
330	2. **Insert-if-absent on the alias.** Why: atomic claim. State: alias is durably owned. What goes wrong: already taken → DB unique-violation → service returns **409 ALIAS_TAKEN**, no overwrite.
331	
332	> 💬 "I don't check-then-claim — I just try to insert and let the database be the referee. The first writer wins, everyone else gets a clean 409."
333	
334	> 🎙️ **Script:** "Custom aliases reuse the create path; the only new thing is correctness. I make the alias the primary key and let the database reject duplicates, so two people racing for the same alias can't both win — the first insert succeeds and the rest get a 409. I keep auto-generated codes in a high integer range so a custom alias can never accidentally collide with a future random one."
335	
336	### 6.4 "Async click analytics"
337	
338	Counting must never touch the hot path. Clicks ride a durable log to a stream processor that aggregates per-code counts. Builds on 6.2's fire-and-forget event.
339	
340	**Architecture decisions table**
341	
342	| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept |
343	|---|---|---|---|---|
344	| Kafka (click log) | A durable append-only log that buffers a firehose of events so nothing is lost if a consumer lags | Decouples the 580K/s hot path from slow analytics; replayable | Not a synchronous DB write per click (kills the hot path); not a fire-into-nowhere UDP (lossy) | Counts are eventually consistent (seconds of lag) |
345	| Stream processor (Flink) | A job that reads the log and maintains rolling counts per code/geo | Stateful windowed aggregation at high throughput | Not a per-event row insert into Postgres (10B/day writes melts it) | Operational complexity of a streaming job |
346	| Analytics store | A columnar/OLAP store + a small KV of current counts for the dashboard | Fast aggregate reads for the stats endpoint | Not the OLTP mapping DB (mixing analytics scans with the hot store) | A second store to keep |
347	
348	> 🎙️ **Narration:** "Every redirect fires a click event onto Kafka — a durable log that buffers the firehose so nothing's lost if the consumer is slow. A Flink job — a streaming processor that keeps running totals in windows — reads the log and maintains counts per code and per country, writing to a columnar analytics store the dashboard reads from. I'd never write a row to the main database on every click; at ten billion clicks a day that would melt it. The trade-off is counts lag a few seconds, which is fine for a dashboard. Events carry a click id so the consumer can dedup retries — at-least-once delivery plus dedup gives me effectively-once counting."
349	
350	> **🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
351	> **Kafka** — a durable, replayable log that holds events until consumers catch up. Here it buffers click events so a slow analytics consumer never slows the redirect.
352	> **Flink** — a stream processor that maintains stateful, windowed aggregates over a firehose. Here it keeps per-code click counts updated continuously.
353	> **OLAP / columnar store** — a database optimized for aggregate scans (sums, group-bys) rather than single-row lookups. Here it answers "clicks by country last week" fast.
354	> **At-least-once + dedup** — the consumer may see an event more than once but ignores duplicates by id. Here it gives effectively-once counts without exactly-once delivery cost.
355	
356	**Diagram — async analytics pipeline**
357	```mermaid

359	    RS[Redirect Service]:::svc
360	    RS -.->|"1 click event"| Kafka[[Kafka click log]]:::async
361	    Kafka -.->|"2 consume"| Flink[Stream processor]:::svc
362	    Flink -->|"3 dedup by click id"| Flink
363	    Flink -->|"4 write counts"| OLAP[(Analytics store)]:::db
364	    Flink -.->|"5 raw events"| ColdStore[(Object store cold)]:::db
365	    Dash[Owner dashboard]:::client -->|"6 GET stats"| StatsSvc[Stats Service]:::svc
366	    StatsSvc -->|"7 read counts"| OLAP
367	    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
368	    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
369	    classDef async fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
370	    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
371	```
372	↳ reuses existing: Redirect Service (from §6.2)
373	
374	**Numbered step narration**
375	1. **Redirect Service fires a click event** onto Kafka (fire-and-forget). State: event durably buffered. What goes wrong: Kafka unavailable → drop locally with a bounded buffer (analytics is lossy-tolerant), redirect still succeeds.
376	2. **Flink consumes** from the log. State: in-flight aggregation. What goes wrong: consumer lag → back-pressure on the log (it retains, doesn't block producers).
377	3. **Dedup by click id** so retries don't double-count. State: idempotent counting.
378	4. **Write rolling counts** to the analytics store. State: dashboard data updated (seconds of lag).
379	5. **Spill raw events** to a cold object store for 90-day retention/replay.
380	6. **Owner GETs stats** via the Stats Service (object-level authz).
381	7. **Stats Service reads pre-aggregated counts.** State: fast aggregate read, no scan of raw events.
382	
383	> 💬 "The redirect throws the click over the wall and forgets about it — counting happens on a separate, replayable pipeline that can fall behind without anyone clicking noticing."
384	
385	> 🎙️ **Script:** "Analytics is fully off the hot path. Every redirect fires an event onto Kafka, a durable log; a Flink stream job reads it, dedups by click id, and keeps rolling counts in an analytics store the dashboard reads from. I never write to the main database on a click — at ten billion a day that's impossible. The cost is counts lag a few seconds and I tolerate at-least-once with dedup, which is exactly the accuracy a dashboard needs."

427	## 7. Data Model & Storage
428	
429	**PART A — Entity table**
430	
431	| Entity | Key fields (PK/shard key annotated) | Chosen store | Shard/partition key | Consistency |
432	|---|---|---|---|---|
433	| ShortLink | **short_code (PK, shard key)**, long_url, owner_id, created_at, expires_at, is_custom | Postgres (sharded) + Redis cache | short_code | Strong on write (unique insert); eventual on cached read |
434	| Counter | range_start (PK), allocated_to | Postgres / etcd (single small store) | n/a (tiny) | Strong (atomic increment) |
435	| ClickEvent | **(short_code, ts) partition**, click_id, geo, referrer, ua | Kafka → OLAP + cold object store | time-partitioned by day | Eventual (at-least-once + dedup) |
436	| Owner | owner_id (PK) | Postgres | owner_id | Strong |
437	
438	**PART B — Storage decision cards**
439	
440	**🗄️ Postgres (sharded) — used for: ShortLink, Owner, Counter**
441	1. **What it is:** a relational database that stores rows with strict rules and guarantees a write either fully succeeds or fully rolls back; here it enforces that a code is unique.
442	2. **Why we picked it:** we need an **atomic insert-if-absent** so two creators racing for the same custom alias can't both win, and a strong PK uniqueness guarantee — only a relational DB gives this cleanly. Writes are light (~6K/s), so a sharded Postgres is plenty.
443	3. **What we considered instead:**
444	   - **DynamoDB** — great KV for the read path, but its conditional writes work; the real reason against it as the *source of truth* is we'd still bolt on a counter scheme and we prefer Postgres's transactional ergonomics + cheaper at this write rate.
445	   - **Cassandra** — excellent write throughput, but last-writer-wins by default makes the alias-uniqueness guarantee awkward (lightweight transactions are slow).
446	   - **One unsharded MySQL** — identical capability to Postgres but 375TB won't fit on one node; we'd shard either way and prefer Postgres tooling.
447	4. **Trade-off we accept:** sharding by code means cross-shard queries (e.g. "all links by owner") need a secondary index or a separate owner→codes table; we denormalize.
448	5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "Mappings live in Postgres sharded by code, because custom aliases need an atomic claim and I want database-enforced uniqueness, not application locks. I looked at Dynamo and Cassandra — fine for the read path — but the uniqueness guarantee is cleanest in Postgres. The cost is cross-shard owner queries, which I solve with a small owner→codes table."
449	
450	**🗄️ Redis (sharded) — used for: hot code → long_url cache**
451	1. **What it is:** an in-memory key-value store with sub-millisecond reads.
452	2. **Why we picked it:** the read path is ~580K/s and the DB can't do that; an in-memory cache sharded across ~4 nodes absorbs it at sub-ms latency.
453	3. **What we considered instead:** **Memcached** — simpler but no persistence/replication if we want warm restarts; **local in-process cache** — can't be shared across many redirect servers and is cold on every deploy.
454	4. **Trade-off we accept:** cache and DB can briefly disagree — fine because mappings are immutable after create (no update path to go stale).
455	5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "Redis holds the hot mappings in memory, sharded because one node tops out near 150K ops a second and I'm at half a million. Mappings never change after creation, so cache staleness basically can't happen."
456	
457	**🗄️ Kafka + OLAP + object store — used for: ClickEvent / analytics**
458	1. **What it is:** Kafka is a durable replayable log; the OLAP store is a columnar DB built for aggregate scans; the object store (S3/Glacier) is cheap cold archival.
459	2. **Why we picked it:** at 10B clicks/day we cannot write a row per click to the OLTP DB; a log buffers the firehose, a stream job aggregates, and the columnar store answers "clicks by country" fast.
460	3. **What we considered instead:** **Postgres per-click insert** — melts at 10B/day; **direct OLAP insert per event** — too many tiny writes, no buffering/replay if the consumer dies.
461	4. **Trade-off we accept:** eventual counts (seconds of lag) and an extra streaming system to operate.
462	5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "Clicks ride Kafka into a Flink job that keeps counts in a columnar store, and raw events spill to cheap object storage. I never touch the main DB on a click. The price is a few seconds of lag, which is exactly fine for a dashboard."
463	
464	**PART C — Per-operation consistency**
465	
466	| Operation | Store | Consistency | Why right here |
467	|---|---|---|---|
468	| Create link / claim alias | Postgres | Strong (atomic unique insert) | Money-adjacent correctness: a collision sends users to the wrong site |
469	| Counter allocate range | Postgres/etcd | Strong (atomic increment) | Two servers must never get the same ID range |
470	| Redirect read (cache hit) | Redis | Eventual (but effectively strong) | Mappings are immutable post-create, so "stale" can't happen |
471	| Redirect read (miss) | Postgres replica | Read replica (ms lag) | Mapping won't change, so lag is harmless |
472	| Read-your-own-writes (just-created link) | Redis/replica | Handled explicitly | After create, the code may not be in cache/replica yet → see below |
473	| Click count read | OLAP | Eventual (seconds) | Dashboard tolerates lag |
474	
475	**Read-your-own-writes:** right after creating a link, the creator might immediately click it before the cache or replica has it. Mitigation: on create we **write-through into Redis** and the create response *is* the proof of existence; if a redirect misses both cache and replica within the replica-lag window, the Redirect Service **reads the primary** for that one lookup. Staleness bound: ≤ replica lag (single-digit ms).
476	
477	**PART D — Data lifecycle (largest table = ClickEvent, ~270TB raw at 90 days)**
478	
479	Raw click events are the fastest-growing table. We keep **90 days of raw events** (tying to the ~270TB / replication-3 figure from §3); beyond that we keep only **rolled-up aggregates** (counts per code/day) which are tiny. Reclamation is **time-partitioned tables with a daily PARTITION DROP** — dropping a day's partition is an O(1) metadata operation, instant, versus a row-by-row DELETE that churns indexes and never cleanly reclaims space. Cold raw events spill to **S3/Glacier** for replay/audit. Without this we'd grow unbounded and the analytics store would dominate cost.
480	
481	> 🎙️ **Script:** "The core is Postgres sharded by code — I chose it for database-enforced uniqueness on custom aliases, since a collision is a correctness disaster. The hot reads are absorbed by sharded Redis, and because mappings are immutable I don't worry about cache staleness. Clicks ride Kafka into Flink and land in a columnar analytics store, with raw events kept 90 days then partition-dropped and archived to S3. I considered Cassandra for the source of truth but its last-writer-wins makes the alias guarantee awkward, so I kept the source of truth relational and used the NoSQL-style stores only where eventual consistency is acceptable."
482

485	## 8. Deep Dives — Bad → Good → Great
486	
487	> 🆘 **If you get stuck:** go back to "this is read-heavy 100:1, codes must be unique by construction, and analytics never touches the hot path" — every answer falls out of those three.

489	### How do we generate short codes that never collide?
490	
491	**#### Bad: Random base62 + check-and-retry**
492	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** roll a random 7-character code, ask the DB "is this taken?", and reroll if it is.
493	- **Approach:** generate 7 random base62 chars, `SELECT` to check existence, insert if free, else retry.
494	- **Why people try this:** it's the obvious first idea — no coordinator needed.
495	- ⚠️ **What breaks:** every write now needs an extra read, and as the keyspace fills, collisions (and retries) climb; under a write burst the retry loop amplifies load on the exact DB you're trying to protect. At ~6K writes/s the extra read isn't fatal yet, but it's an unbounded-retry latent risk and it makes uniqueness probabilistic.
496	- 🔁 **Forces upgrade:** the need for *guaranteed* uniqueness with no per-write read pushes us to a counter.
497	
498	**#### Good: Single global counter + base62**
499	- ↩️ **What the previous tier got wrong:** random codes need a DB check on every write; a counter is unique *by construction*, so no check, no retry.
500	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** hand out an ever-increasing number like a deli ticket and base62-encode it.
501	- **Approach:** a single atomic counter (a row with `FOR UPDATE`, or a Redis `INCR`) gives the next integer; base62-encode it into the code.
502	- **Why people try this:** it's correct and simple to reason about.
503	- ⚠️ **What breaks:** the counter is now a **global coordinator** — every create serializes through it. At ~6K writes/s a single `INCR` is fine, but its *availability* is now the write tier's availability, and codes are **sequential/guessable** (you can enumerate other people's links).
504	- 🔁 **Forces upgrade:** the single point of serialization + guessability pushes us to ranged allocation + obfuscation.
505	
506	```mermaid
507	flowchart TD
508	    WS[Write Service]:::svc -->|"1 next id"| Counter[(Atomic counter)]:::db
509	    Counter -->|"2 id = N"| WS
510	    WS -->|"3 base62 encode"| WS
511	    WS -->|"4 insert mapping"| Mapping[(Mapping store)]:::db
512	    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
513	    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
514	```
515	
516	**#### Great: Ranged KGS + per-server blocks (+ optional bit-permutation)**
517	- ↩️ **What the previous tier got wrong:** a per-write counter call serializes every create and is a single availability dependency; granting *ranges* removes ~99.9% of those calls, and permuting bits removes guessability.
518	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** the counter hands each server a block of a thousand numbers at once, so servers mint codes locally and barely ever talk to the counter; then we scramble the bits so codes aren't sequential.
519	- **Approach:** the **KGS** atomically advances the counter by 1,000 and grants `[N, N+999]` to a server. The server mints codes locally from its block. To kill guessability, we apply a reversible **bit permutation** (a Feistel/XOR scramble — a deterministic two-way shuffle of the integer's bits) *before* base62 encoding, so consecutive IDs produce non-adjacent codes. Custom aliases bypass the counter and use insert-if-absent in the same namespace partition.
520	- 🔢 **Decision-forcing math:** per-write counter = 6K coordinator calls/s; ranged at 1,000/block = ~6 calls/s — a **1000x** reduction, so the coordinator is no longer a hot spot and a brief KGS outage just means servers run on their current block. Keyspace: 62^7 ≈ 3.5T ≫ our 182B over 5 years, so no exhaustion.
521	- ✅ **Failure matrix:**
522	
523	| Scenario | What happens |
524	|---|---|
525	| Server crashes mid-block | Remaining IDs in the block are burned (lost) — harmless, 3.5T keyspace |
526	| KGS down briefly | Servers keep minting from their current block; new blocks paused → degrade, not fail |
527	| Two servers request blocks simultaneously | Atomic counter increment serializes them → disjoint ranges, no overlap |
528	| Custom alias collides with auto code | Can't — auto codes come from high counter integers + permutation; aliases gated by denylist |
529	| Counter store lost | Restore from durable storage; worst case re-grant from `max(existing code)` — never reuse a granted range |
530	
531	```mermaid
532	flowchart TD
533	    WS1[Write Service A]:::svc -->|"1 request block"| KGS[KGS]:::svc
534	    WS2[Write Service B]:::svc -->|"1 request block"| KGS
535	    KGS -->|"2 atomic add 1000"| Counter[(Counter store)]:::db
536	    KGS -->|"3a grant 1-1000"| WS1
537	    KGS -->|"3b grant 1001-2000"| WS2
538	    WS1 -->|"4 permute then base62 local"| WS1
539	    WS1 -->|"5 insert"| Mapping[(Mapping store)]:::db
540	    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
541	    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
542	```
543	
544	> 🎙️ **Script:** "I generate codes from a counter, not randomly, so uniqueness is guaranteed by construction — no check, no retry. To stop the counter being a hot spot, the key-generation service hands each server a block of a thousand IDs, so it's hit about six times a second instead of six thousand. I scramble the bits with a reversible permutation before base62-encoding, so codes don't run in sequence and you can't guess your neighbor's link. If a server dies, it just wastes a block — with three-and-a-half trillion codes that's free."
545	
546	> 🧠 **If they ask "why not just hash the URL?":** "Hashing the same URL twice gives the same code, which is nice for dedup, but different URLs can hash to the same prefix — a collision — so you're back to checking and retrying. The counter never collides, so I keep it."
547	
548	### How do we serve ~580K redirects/s and survive a cold cache?
549	
550	**#### Good: Single Redis + replicas behind it**
551	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** keep all hot mappings in one big in-memory cache, fall back to the DB on a miss.
552	- **Approach:** Redirect Service reads Redis; miss → Postgres replica → backfill.
553	- ⚠️ **What breaks:** one Redis node caps near **150K ops/s**; we're at ~580K/s, so a single node is ~4x over. And on a cold cache (deploy/flush), all ~580K/s hit the DB at once — replicas melt.
554	- 🔁 **Forces upgrade:** the per-node ceiling forces sharding; the stampede forces request coalescing.
555	
556	**#### Great: Sharded Redis + CDN + single-flight + hot-key replication**
557	- ↩️ **What the previous tier got wrong:** one node can't do 580K/s and a cold cache stampedes the DB; sharding spreads the load, single-flight collapses the stampede, and hot-key replication handles a viral code.
558	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** spread the cache across several nodes, put a CDN in front, and when a popular code misses, let *one* request fetch it while the rest wait for that result.
559	- **Approach:** shard Redis by code across ~4 nodes (each ~150K/s). A **CDN** edge-caches the 302 briefly to shave origin load. **Single-flight** *(coalescing — only the first miss for a key goes to the DB; concurrent misses for the same key wait on it)* kills the stampede. For a **celebrity/hot code** (one viral link taking a huge share of traffic), that one key could exceed a single shard's capacity, so we **replicate the hot key to all shards** (or rely on the CDN + local micro-cache) so no single shard is the bottleneck.
560	- 🔢 **Decision-forcing math:** 580K/s ÷ 150K/node ≈ 4 nodes (+1 headroom). Cold-cache worst case = full 580K/s to DB; with single-flight, the DB sees at most one read per *distinct* code per refill, not per request — collapsing a stampede of millions into the number of unique hot codes (~thousands).
561	- ✅ **Failure matrix:**
562	
563	| Scenario | What happens |
564	|---|---|
565	| One Redis shard dies | Its key range misses → falls to replica; single-flight prevents stampede; we re-shard / promote a replica |
566	| Cold cache after deploy | Single-flight + CDN cap DB load; cache warms in seconds |
567	| Viral/celebrity code | Hot-key replicated across shards + CDN edge cache absorbs it |
568	| All cache down | Redirect Service reads replicas directly; latency rises but redirects still work (degraded) |
569

571	flowchart TD
572	    RS[Redirect Service]:::svc -->|"1 read code"| Router[Shard router]:::svc
573	    Router -->|"2 shard by code"| R1[(Redis shard 1)]:::db
574	    Router -->|"2 shard by code"| R2[(Redis shard 2)]:::db
575	    RS -.->|"3 single-flight on miss"| SF[Coalescer]:::svc
576	    SF -->|"4 one read per key"| Replica[(Postgres replica)]:::db
577	    Replica -->|"5 backfill"| R1
578	    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
579	    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
580	```
581	
582	> 🎙️ **Script:** "Half a million reads a second is past one cache node, so I shard Redis by code across about four nodes and put a CDN in front. The scary case is a cold cache or a viral link: a stampede where everyone misses the same key at once. I collapse it with single-flight — only the first miss for a code goes to the database, everyone else waits on that one result — and for a true celebrity link I replicate that one hot key across all shards so no single shard is the bottleneck. Even with the whole cache gone, redirects still work off replicas, just slower."
583	
584	> 🧠 **If they ask "301 or 302?":** "302 by default — a temporary redirect the browser comes back to us for, so I keep seeing every click for analytics and can repoint the link. 301 would let browsers cache it and cut my traffic and cost, but I'd go blind on clicks. It's a one-line product decision."
585	
586	### How do we count clicks without slowing redirects, and not lose or double-count them?
587	
588	**#### Good: Fire event onto a queue, increment a counter**
589	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** throw each click onto a queue and have a worker bump a count.
590	- **Approach:** Redirect fires to Kafka; a consumer increments a per-code counter.
591	- ⚠️ **What breaks:** retries (consumer crash → reprocess) **double-count**; a naive synchronous counter on a viral code is itself a hot key.
592	- 🔁 **Forces upgrade:** the need for dedup + windowed aggregation forces a stream processor with idempotent state.
593	
594	**#### Great: Kafka → Flink windowed + dedup by click_id → OLAP**
595	- ↩️ **What the previous tier got wrong:** at-least-once delivery double-counts on retry; a single counter per hot code is a write hot-spot; Flink dedups by click id and aggregates in windows.
596	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** every click carries a unique id; the stream job ignores ids it's already seen and keeps rolling totals, so retries don't inflate counts and one viral link doesn't hammer a single counter.
597	- **Approach:** each ClickEvent has a `click_id`. Flink keys by code, maintains windowed counts, and **dedups on `click_id`** within a window; results land in the OLAP store. Back-pressure: Kafka **retains** rather than blocking producers; if a consumer lags past a threshold we scale consumers or shed the lowest-value enrichment (e.g. geo lookup) first, never the redirect.
598	- 🔢 **Decision-forcing math:** 10B events/day ÷ 86,400 ≈ 116K events/s average, ×5 peak ≈ 580K/s — same as reads (one event per click). A row-per-click into Postgres at 580K/s is impossible; Kafka + Flink handles it and the OLAP store only sees aggregates.
599	- ✅ **Failure matrix:**
600	
601	| Scenario | What happens |
602	|---|---|
603	| Consumer crashes, reprocesses | Dedup by click_id → no double count |
604	| Kafka briefly down | Redirect drops event into a bounded local buffer → minor analytics loss, redirect unaffected |
605	| Consumer lag spikes | Kafka retains; scale consumers; shed enrichment before dropping events |
606	| Hot code (viral) | Per-key windowed aggregation spreads work; no single synchronous counter |
607	
608	```mermaid
609	flowchart TD
610	    RS[Redirect Service]:::svc -.->|"1 click_id event"| Kafka[[Kafka]]:::async
611	    Kafka -.->|"2 consume"| Flink[Flink windowed]:::svc
612	    Flink -->|"3 dedup click_id"| Flink
613	    Flink -->|"4 rolling counts"| OLAP[(Analytics store)]:::db
614	    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
615	    classDef async fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
616	    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
617	```
618	
619	> 🎙️ **Script:** "Counting rides Kafka, a durable log, so the redirect just fires and forgets. A Flink job dedups each click by a unique click id and keeps windowed counts, so even if a consumer crashes and replays, I don't double-count. If the consumer falls behind, Kafka holds the events and I scale consumers or drop the geo enrichment first — I never let analytics push back on the redirect. The whole thing is at-least-once plus dedup, which is effectively-once at a fraction of the cost."
620	
621	> 🧠 **If they ask "what if you need exact billable counts?":** "Then I harden it — durable ingest with replay, idempotent consumers keyed on click_id, and a nightly reconciliation pass against the raw event log in S3 to true up the aggregates."

624	
625	## 9. Reliability, Failure Modes & Cost
626	
627	**9A — Availability targets.** Redirect path target **99.99% ("four nines" = ~52 min/year of downtime total)**; create path **99.9% ("three nines" = ~8.7 hours/year)** since a create can wait but a redirect can't. Mechanisms: **CDN** (absorbs traffic near users), **sharded Redis + multiple replicas**, and **multi-AZ** *(multiple data centres in one region so if one building loses power another takes over automatically)* for the DB and cache.
628	
629	**9B — Per-dependency failure table**
630	
631	| Component | What breaks if down | Graceful degradation (what the USER sees) | Recovery |
632	|---|---|---|---|
633	| Redis (cache) | Hot reads lose their fast path | Redirects still work via replicas, just slower (tens of ms) — users barely notice | Re-warm via single-flight; promote replica shard |
634	| Postgres primary | New creates fail | Existing redirects unaffected (immutable, cached); "try again" on create only | Multi-AZ failover to standby |
635	| Postgres replica | Cache misses can't be served | Single-flight + read-from-primary fallback; users see normal redirects | Replace replica from primary |
636	| KGS / counter | New auto-code creates pause | Servers mint from their current block; redirects unaffected | Restart KGS; never reuse granted ranges |
637	| Kafka / Flink | Analytics ingestion stalls | Counts go stale; redirects fully unaffected | Kafka retains + replay on recovery |
638	| CDN | More traffic hits origin | Slightly higher latency; origin sized with headroom absorbs it | CDN auto-recovers; origin autoscale |
639	
640	**9C — RPO / RTO.** **RPO (Recovery Point Objective)** = how much data we'd lose if we crashed right now; **RTO (Recovery Time Objective)** = how long until we're back.
641	
642	| Data class | RPO | RTO | Mechanism |
643	|---|---|---|---|
644	| Mappings (ShortLink) | ≈ 0 (sync replicated before ack) | ~1-2 min | Multi-AZ sync standby + WAL |
645	| Counter state | ≈ 0 | ~1 min | Durable + never-reuse-range invariant |
646	| Click analytics | minutes (lossy-tolerant) | minutes | Kafka retention + replay; aggregates rebuildable from S3 raw |
647	
648	**9D — Cost.** Top 3 drivers (rough monthly, computed in `capacity.py`): (1) **Redirect egress + load balancer ~$150K/mo** — serving ~300B tiny redirect responses a month; this is the *biggest* line, not the DB. (2) **Analytics pipeline ~$25K/mo** — Kafka + Flink + columnar store + S3. (3) **DB + cache fleet ~$18K/mo** combined. **Total ~$190K/mo, ~$0.64 per 1M redirects.** Biggest optimization: flip popular links to **301 + aggressive CDN caching** so browsers/edges absorb redirects and we stop paying origin egress — directly attacks the dominant cost (at the price of click visibility).
649	
650	**Back-pressure (hottest async path = clicks):** Kafka is a **bounded, retaining** log — producers never block; if consumer lag crosses a threshold we **scale consumers**, then **shed enrichment** (geo/UA lookups), and only as a last resort drop raw events — the redirect itself never back-pressures. **Observability:** golden signals — **latency** (redirect p99), **traffic** (redirect QPS), **errors** (4xx/5xx, miss rate), **saturation** (Redis ops, Kafka lag); a **burn-rate alert** fires if we'd exhaust the four-nines error budget; a **trace-id** propagates gateway → redirect service → Kafka → Flink so one click is traceable across the async hop. **Rate-limiting:** the create endpoint uses a **token-bucket** limiter (state in Redis — itself a global coordinator), returning **429 + Retry-After** on abuse; redirects are not rate-limited per user (they're public) but are protected by the CDN + WAF.
651	
652	> 🎙️ **Script:** "If Redis dies, users still get redirected off the replicas — just a bit slower; they probably never notice. If the database primary fails, existing links keep working because they're immutable and cached, only new creates pause while we fail over to the standby. If Kafka or Flink stalls, the dashboard goes stale but not a single redirect is affected. And the thing that surprises people: the biggest cost isn't the database, it's the bandwidth and load balancers for billions of tiny redirects — so the cheapest big win is flipping hot links to 301 and letting the CDN serve them."
653

656	## 10. Trade-off Ledger
657	
658	**🔀 Decision: 302 (temporary) vs 301 (permanent) redirect**
659	1. **Chose & why:** 302 — every click comes back to us, so analytics works and we can repoint links.
660	2. **Gave up:** lower traffic/cost (301 lets browsers cache and skip us).
661	3. 🗣️ **Plain words:** like telling people "ask me each time" instead of "memorize this address" — I stay in the loop but answer more calls.
662	4. **When this reverses:** if analytics leaves scope or cost/SEO dominate, flip to 301 + CDN and accept click blindness.
663	5. 🗣️ **How to say it:** "I default to 302 to keep analytics real; if you don't need click data, 301 cuts my biggest cost."
664	
665	**🔀 Decision: Counter + KGS vs random/hash codes**
666	1. **Chose & why:** counter + ranged KGS — uniqueness by construction, no per-write check.
667	2. **Gave up:** the simplicity of a coordinator-free random generator.
668	3. 🗣️ **Plain words:** a deli-ticket dispenser instead of everyone guessing a number and checking if it's free.
669	4. **When this reverses:** if a tiny collision-retry rate is acceptable and we want zero coordination, switch to random base62 + check.
670	5. 🗣️ **How to say it:** "Counter so codes never collide and I never check on write; random is simpler if you tolerate the occasional retry."
671	
672	**🔀 Decision: Eventual analytics (at-least-once + dedup) vs exact counting**
673	1. **Chose & why:** eventual — keeps the hot path untouched and the pipeline cheap.
674	2. **Gave up:** exact, real-time, billable-grade counts.
675	3. 🗣️ **Plain words:** a fast approximate scoreboard instead of an audited ledger.
676	4. **When this reverses:** if clicks become billable, harden to exactly-once + reconciliation.
677	5. 🗣️ **How to say it:** "Counts lag a few seconds and that's fine for a dashboard; if we charge per click I'd add reconciliation."
678	
679	**🔀 Decision: Sharded Redis cache vs DB-only reads**
680	1. **Chose & why:** sharded Redis — only way to hit sub-ms at ~580K/s.
681	2. **Gave up:** operational simplicity of one store.
682	3. 🗣️ **Plain words:** a wall of fast memory in front of the slow filing cabinet.
683	4. **When this reverses:** if peak reads drop below ~150K/s, collapse to one cache node (the flip threshold).
684	5. 🗣️ **How to say it:** "Reads are too heavy for the DB, so a sharded in-memory cache carries them; below ~150K/s I'd drop to a single node."
685	
686	**CAP/PACELC:** the redirect read path is **AP** (we favor availability — serve a possibly-slightly-stale mapping, though mappings are immutable so it's effectively consistent). The create/alias-claim write path is **CP** (we favor consistency — a unique insert must serialize). **PACELC:** else (normal operation) we favor **latency** on reads and **consistency** on writes.
687	
688	> 🎙️ **Script:** "The decisions I'd flag: 302 over 301 to keep analytics, which costs me traffic; a counter over random codes for guaranteed uniqueness, which adds a coordinator I had to make cheap with ranges; eventual analytics, which I'd harden only if clicks become billable; and a sharded cache, which I'd collapse if traffic ever fell below one node's ceiling. The read path is AP and effectively consistent because mappings never change; the write path is CP because uniqueness has to serialize."
689
