692	## 11. Likely Interviewer Questions & Answers
693	
694	**❓ "Two users request the same custom alias at the same instant — who wins?"**
695	*Mechanism:* both go to insert the alias as the primary key; the DB's unique constraint serializes them, so the first commit wins and the second gets a unique-violation we surface as **409 ALIAS_TAKEN**. No application lock, no read-then-write race.
696	🗣️ *Plain words:* the database is the referee — first to write the alias keeps it, the other is told it's taken.
697	💬 *One-liner:* "I let the unique constraint be the referee, so two racers can't both win."
698	
699	**❓ "Why a counter instead of hashing the URL or random codes?"**
700	*Mechanism:* hashing and random both can produce the same code for different URLs (a collision), forcing a DB check-and-retry on every write; a counter is monotonic so codes are unique by construction with no check.
701	🗣️ *Plain words:* random can clash and make me re-roll; a counter never clashes.
702	💬 *One-liner:* "Counter is unique by construction — no per-write check, no retry storm."
703	
704	**❓ "Isn't the counter a single bottleneck and a single point of failure?"**
705	*Mechanism:* the KGS grants ranges of 1,000 IDs, so the counter is touched ~6 times/s, not 6K; if KGS is briefly down, servers keep minting from their current block, so it degrades rather than fails.
706	🗣️ *Plain words:* servers grab a thousand numbers at once, so they almost never need the counter.
707	💬 *One-liner:* "Ranged allocation turns a per-write coordinator into a once-per-thousand nudge."
708	
709	**❓ "What happens on a cold cache or a viral link — a stampede on one key?"**
710	*Mechanism:* single-flight (request coalescing) sends only the first miss for a code to the DB while concurrent misses wait on that result; a celebrity code is replicated across all cache shards and edge-cached by the CDN so no single shard is the bottleneck.
711	🗣️ *Plain words:* when everyone clicks the same hot link, one request fetches it and the rest reuse that answer.
712	💬 *One-liner:* "Single-flight collapses a million misses into one DB read per code."
713	
714	**❓ "What if Redis goes down entirely?"**
715	*Mechanism:* the Redirect Service falls back to Postgres read replicas; latency rises from sub-ms to tens of ms but redirects still resolve, then the cache re-warms via single-flight.
716	🗣️ *Plain words:* we read from the database copies instead — slower, but links still work.
717	💬 *One-liner:* "Cache loss is a latency event, not an outage — replicas still answer."
718	
719	**❓ "How do you handle a flash crowd — 5x normal traffic on a viral link?"**
720	*Mechanism:* the CDN absorbs edge-cacheable redirects, sharded Redis spreads the rest across nodes, and hot-key replication keeps one code from overloading a single shard; we sized ~4 cache nodes for the 580K/s peak with headroom.
721	🗣️ *Plain words:* the work spreads across the CDN and several cache nodes so no one box drowns.
722	💬 *One-liner:* "CDN plus a sharded cache plus hot-key replication — no single shard is a choke point."
723	
724	**❓ "Can the same link be created twice / can a retry mint two codes?"**
725	*Mechanism:* the create carries an `Idempotency-Key`; a retried request with the same key returns the original code instead of minting a new one, deduped via a short-TTL idempotency store.
726	🗣️ *Plain words:* if your phone retries the request, you get the same short link back, not a second one.
727	💬 *One-liner:* "Idempotency key means a retry is a no-op that returns the first result."
728	
729	**❓ "Can you oversell the keyspace — run out of codes?"**
730	*Mechanism:* 62^7 ≈ 3.5 trillion codes vs ~182 billion links in 5 years — about 1.5% utilized; if it ever filled we'd extend to 8 chars (218T) transparently for new codes.
731	🗣️ *Plain words:* seven characters give trillions of codes; we use a tiny sliver.
732	💬 *One-liner:* "We're at ~1.5% of a 3.5-trillion keyspace — exhaustion isn't a real risk."
733	
734	**❓ "How do you stop someone forging links as another user, or grabbing reserved paths?"**
735	*Mechanism:* `ownerId` comes from the JWT, never the request body, so you can't create links as someone else; custom aliases pass a reserved denylist (`api`, `admin`, `login`) and object-level authz gates stats reads (`caller.sub == link.ownerId`).
736	🗣️ *Plain words:* we trust the signed token for who you are, not anything you type in the request.
737	💬 *One-liner:* "Owner comes from the token, never the body — forging is impossible."
738	
739	**❓ "How do you prevent malicious URLs / abuse?"**
740	*Mechanism:* create is rate-limited (token bucket, 429 + Retry-After), URLs are validated and checked against a denylist/safe-browsing feed asynchronously, and we can disable a code (flip it to a warning page) without deleting it.
741	🗣️ *Plain words:* we throttle creators, screen destinations, and can switch off a bad link instantly.
742	💬 *One-liner:* "Rate-limit creates, screen destinations, and keep a kill switch per code."
743	
744	**❓ "Can a redirect ever send a user to the wrong site?"**
745	*Mechanism:* mappings are immutable after create and the code is the primary key, so a cached value can't be stale-wrong; the only way to a wrong site is a collision, which the counter prevents by construction.
746	🗣️ *Plain words:* a code points to exactly one URL forever, so it can't drift to the wrong place.
747	💬 *One-liner:* "Immutable mappings + unique codes mean a redirect is never wrong."
748	
749	**❓ "What's the most expensive part, and how would you cut it?"**
750	*Mechanism:* the dominant cost is redirect egress + load balancers (~$150K/mo of ~$190K), not the DB; flipping popular links to 301 + CDN caching offloads those redirects to the edge/browser.
751	🗣️ *Plain words:* serving billions of tiny redirects costs more than storing the data.
752	💬 *One-liner:* "Bandwidth dwarfs the DB — push hot links to 301 + CDN to cut it."
753	
754	**❓ "Why is analytics eventually consistent — isn't that wrong?"**
755	*Mechanism:* clicks ride Kafka into a Flink job that dedups by `click_id` and keeps windowed counts; counts lag seconds but never block the redirect, and dedup makes at-least-once delivery effectively-once.
756	🗣️ *Plain words:* the scoreboard updates a few seconds late so the redirect stays instant.
757	💬 *One-liner:* "Counts lag seconds by design so nothing slows the click."
758	
759	**❓ "How would you add link expiry / TTL?"**
760	*Mechanism:* `expires_at` is already in the model; the Redirect Service checks it (cheap, already in cache) and returns 410 Gone if past; a partition-drop job reclaims expired rows in bulk.
761	🗣️ *Plain words:* each link can have an end date; after that it shows a "link expired" page.
762	💬 *One-liner:* "Expiry is a field check on read plus a partition-drop cleanup — no per-row deletes."
763	
764	**❓ "How would you support multi-region / global low latency?"**
765	*Mechanism:* mappings are immutable, so we replicate them read-only to every region and serve redirects locally; creates route to a single home region (single-write-region) to keep the counter authoritative, with cross-region async replication.
766	🗣️ *Plain words:* reads happen near the user everywhere; new links are minted in one home region.
767	💬 *One-liner:* "Reads go global because mappings never change; writes stay single-region for the counter."
768	
769	**❓ "How would you handle right-to-erasure / deleting a link's data?"**
770	*Mechanism:* soft-delete the mapping (flip to 410), purge it from cache, and crypto-shred the owner linkage; raw click events age out via partition-drop and the cold S3 copy is deleted on request.
771	🗣️ *Plain words:* we turn the link off, clear the caches, and let the analytics data age out and get purged.
772	💬 *One-liner:* "Disable the code, purge caches, and let partition-drop plus S3 deletion finish erasure."
773	
774	> 🎙️ **60-second verbal summary:** "A URL shortener takes a long link and hands back a tiny code that redirects to the original, and it's read-heavy by about a hundred to one. The design splits into two halves. The read half is the whole product: clicks hit a CDN, then a stateless redirect service, then a sharded in-memory Redis cache that answers in under a millisecond, with only the ~5% of misses falling to Postgres read replicas — and I emit a 302 so I keep seeing every click. The write half mints short codes from a counter, not random or hashed strings, so they're unique by construction; a key-generation service hands each server a block of a thousand IDs so the counter is barely touched, and custom aliases use an atomic unique insert so two creators can't both win. The two halves connect only through a fire-and-forget click event onto Kafka, where a Flink job dedups and counts asynchronously, so analytics can never slow a redirect. Every hard decision traces back to one principle: protect the read hot-path and guarantee code uniqueness by construction, and push everything else off to the side."

The capacity table at line 99-102 sizes Redis from full `read_qps_peak` (580K/s) NOT discounting for CDN offload. So the math is internally consistent with a "no CDN offload" story already — the Redis fleet is sized for the full 580K/s, and the egress cost assumes all reads reach origin.

This means the **coherent story that requires NO math change** is: keep `private, max-age=0` (full per-click analytics) and stop claiming the CDN offloads redirects. The CDN's value is then TLS termination, DDoS/WAF protection, and proxy-hop latency, not cache-hit offload.

The design's entire thesis is "we obsess over per-click analytics" (302, every click observable, line 33, 60, 78, 273). Choosing CDN cache offload would contradict that core thesis. So the correct fix is: **keep `private, max-age=0`, and fix the four CDN-offload claims** to be about TLS/DDoS/latency, not cache offload.

HTTP/1.1 302 Found
Location: https://example.com/very/long/path?utm=spring

```
- **Key decision — 302 vs 301 AND why `private, max-age=0`:** I return **302 Found** *(temporary redirect — the browser comes back to us next time)* by default, and I deliberately set `Cache-Control: private, max-age=0` so **no shared cache (CDN) and not even the browser ever caches the redirect** — every single click reaches the origin and is observable for analytics, and we can repoint the link later. **The cost of this choice is explicit: the CDN delivers zero redirect-cache offload — it still earns its place for TLS termination, DDoS/WAF protection, and a closer proxy hop, but every redirect is sized to hit our origin (the capacity model assumes the full 580K/s reaches us).** The alternative — `public, max-age=<small N>` — would let the CDN edge-cache the 302 and shave origin load, but we'd go blind to every click within that window, so we don't do it by default; it's the per-product flip (Fork B, same axis as 301). For a 404 (unknown code) we return a small branded page, not a stack trace.

content.content:
76:  - If SEO/permanence and offloading traffic from our servers matter more than per-click analytics → use **301 (permanent)**: browsers and intermediaries cache it, so we serve fewer redirects, but we *stop seeing most clicks*.
176:- **Key decision — 302 vs 301 AND why `private, max-age=0`:** I return **302 Found** *(temporary redirect — the browser comes back to us next time)* by default, and I deliberately set `Cache-Control: private, max-age=0` so **no shared cache (CDN) and not even the browser ever caches the redirect** — every single click reaches the origin and is observable for analytics, and we can repoint the link later. **The cost of this choice is explicit: the CDN delivers zero redirect-cache offload — it still earns its place for TLS termination, DDoS/WAF protection, and a closer proxy hop, but every redirect is sized to hit our origin (the capacity model assumes the full 580K/s reaches us).** The alternative — `public, max-age=<small N>` — would let the CDN edge-cache the 302 and shave origin load, but we'd go blind to every click within that window, so we don't do it by default; it's the per-product flip (Fork B, same axis as 301). For a 404 (unknown code) we return a small branded page, not a stack trace.
262:| CDN / edge | Geographically distributed PoPs in front of the origin — TLS termination, DDoS/WAF scrubbing, and a near-user proxy hop | Cuts handshake/RTT latency and absorbs attack traffic close to the user; keeps a warm origin connection | Not origin-only (every TLS handshake and attack packet would cross the internet to us) | With `private, max-age=0` the edge does NOT cache the 302 — zero redirect offload, so the full 580K/s still reaches origin; we accept that to keep per-click analytics |
267:> 🎙️ **Narration:** "On a click, I want to never touch the database. The request hits a CDN — but note its job here is TLS termination, DDoS/WAF scrubbing, and a near-user proxy hop, NOT caching the redirect: I set `private, max-age=0` so every click reaches origin for analytics, which means the CDN gives me zero redirect-cache offload and the full half-million-a-second still lands on us. From the CDN it's a stateless redirect service, then Redis, an in-memory cache. Redis is in-memory so reads are sub-millisecond, and I shard it because one node tops out around a hundred-fifty thousand ops a second and I'm at half a million. Only the ~5% of reads that miss go to a Postgres read replica — that's about thirty thousand a second, which three replicas handle. I emit a 302 so the browser comes back next time and I keep seeing clicks. The trade-off is the cache and DB can disagree for a moment, but mappings are immutable once created, so that's a non-issue."
307:> 🎙️ **Script:** "A click hits a CDN, then a stateless redirect service, then Redis — an in-memory cache that answers in under a millisecond. About 95% of reads are served from cache; only misses fall through to a Postgres read replica, which then backfills the cache. I emit a 302 so the browser keeps coming back and I keep seeing clicks, and I fire the click event onto a queue without waiting for it. The one decision that makes this slice work is sharding the cache, because one node can't do half a million reads a second."
559:- **Approach:** shard Redis by code across ~4 nodes (each ~150K/s). A **CDN** sits in front for TLS termination and DDoS/WAF scrubbing — note it does **not** cache the 302 (we send `private, max-age=0` to keep per-click analytics), so it gives zero redirect offload and the full read load still reaches origin; sharded Redis is what actually carries the 580K/s. **Single-flight** *(coalescing — only the first miss for a key goes to the DB; concurrent misses for the same key wait on it)* kills the stampede. For a **celebrity/hot code** (one viral link taking a huge share of traffic), that one key could exceed a single shard's capacity, so we **replicate the hot key to all shards** (and use a short-lived local micro-cache on the redirect servers) so no single shard is the bottleneck.
638:| CDN | Origin loses edge TLS termination + DDoS/WAF scrubbing (redirect volume is unchanged — CDN never cached it) | Slightly higher latency and less attack protection; origin sized with headroom and its own WAF absorbs it | CDN auto-recovers; origin autoscale |
648:**9D — Cost.** Top 3 drivers (rough monthly, computed in `capacity.py`): (1) **Redirect egress + load balancer ~$150K/mo** — serving ~300B tiny redirect responses a month; this is the *biggest* line, not the DB. (2) **Analytics pipeline ~$25K/mo** — Kafka + Flink + columnar store + S3. (3) **DB + cache fleet ~$18K/mo** combined. **Total ~$190K/mo, ~$0.64 per 1M redirects.** Biggest optimization: flip popular links to **301 + aggressive CDN caching** so browsers/edges absorb redirects and we stop paying origin egress — directly attacks the dominant cost (at the price of click visibility).
652:> 🎙️ **Script:** "If Redis dies, users still get redirected off the replicas — just a bit slower; they probably never notice. If the database primary fails, existing links keep working because they're immutable and cached, only new creates pause while we fail over to the standby. If Kafka or Flink stalls, the dashboard goes stale but not a single redirect is affected. And the thing that surprises people: the biggest cost isn't the database, it's the bandwidth and load balancers for billions of tiny redirects — so the cheapest big win is flipping hot links to 301 and letting the CDN serve them."
720:*Mechanism:* sharded Redis spreads the read load across nodes and hot-key replication (plus a short-lived local micro-cache on the redirect servers) keeps one viral code from overloading a single shard; we sized ~4 cache nodes for the full 580K/s peak with headroom, since we send `private, max-age=0` and the CDN does not cache redirects — the CDN's role in a flash crowd is scrubbing DDoS/WAF traffic and terminating TLS at the edge, not absorbing click load.
750:*Mechanism:* the dominant cost is redirect egress + load balancers (~$150K/mo of ~$190K), not the DB; flipping popular links to 301 + CDN caching offloads those redirects to the edge/browser.
774:> 🎙️ **60-second verbal summary:** "A URL shortener takes a long link and hands back a tiny code that redirects to the original, and it's read-heavy by about a hundred to one. The design splits into two halves. The read half is the whole product: clicks hit a CDN, then a stateless redirect service, then a sharded in-memory Redis cache that answers in under a millisecond, with only the ~5% of misses falling to Postgres read replicas — and I emit a 302 so I keep seeing every click. The write half mints short codes from a counter, not random or hashed strings, so they're unique by construction; a key-generation service hands each server a block of a thousand IDs so the counter is barely touched, and custom aliases use an atomic unique insert so two creators can't both win. The two halves connect only through a fire-and-forget click event onto Kafka, where a Flink job dedups and counts asynchronously, so analytics can never slow a redirect. Every hard decision traces back to one principle: protect the read hot-path and guarantee code uniqueness by construction, and push everything else off to the side."

I've fixed the MAJOR internal contradiction in the HLD session. Here's a summary:

## What was wrong
The redirect contract emitted `Cache-Control: private, max-age=0` (which forbids ALL caching by the CDN and browser), yet the design repeatedly claimed the CDN edge-caches the 302 to offload origin traffic (lines 262, 559, 720). With that header the CDN delivers zero redirect offload, so the stated purpose of the CDN tier contradicted the chosen header.

## How I fixed it
I picked the coherent story that matches the design's own thesis (302 + full per-click analytics) AND the existing capacity math: **kept `private, max-age=0`, and stopped claiming the CDN offloads redirects.** The CDN's value is now reframed as TLS termination, DDoS/WAF scrubbing, and a near-user proxy hop.

## Key finding on capacity.py — no change needed
I ran capacity.py with the project venv. The Redis fleet (4 nodes) is sized from the full 580K/s read peak, and the $150K egress cost is computed on all 300B reqs/mo — i.e. the math **already** assumes zero CDN offload. Keeping the header is what makes the math consistent; editing it would have introduced a contradiction.

## Edits (all in _response.md)
- §5.2: expanded the redirect key-decision to explain the header forbids all caching, names the `public, max-age=N` alternative as the explicit flip (cost = click-blindness in the window).
- §6.2 table/narration/steps + the §6.2 mermaid edge labels: CDN reframed as TLS/DDoS, edge labels updated to "no cache".
- §8 Great-tier approach, two failure-matrix rows, and script: removed "edge-caches the 302 to shave origin load"; sharded Redis carries the load.
- §11 flash-crowd + stampede answers: removed "CDN absorbs edge-cacheable redirects".
- §9A and §9B failure table: CDN failure now correctly says redirect volume is unchanged (it never cached them).

Left the 301-flip mentions (Fork B, §9D, §10, §11 cost answer) untouched — those legitimately describe the *alternative* 301+CDN-caching mode.
